content.problemStatement:
Rupsa really loves triangles. One day she came across an equilateral triangle having length of each side as an integer **N**. She started wondering if it was possible to transform the triangle keeping two sides fixed and alter the third side such that it still remains a triangle, but the altered side will have its length as an even integer, and the line drawn from the opposite vertex to the mid-point of the altered side is of integral length.

Since Rupsa is in a hurry to record a song for Chef as he really loves her songs, you must help her solve the problem as fast as possible.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains a single integer N.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing "YES" if it is possible to perform the described transformation, otherwise output "NO".

sampleTestCases.explanation: Both N=5 and N=3 are greater than 1, therefore a suitable even side length exists and the answer is YES for each.
N=4 > 1, so the transformation is possible.
For N=1 no even integer can satisfy the triangle inequality, hence the answer is NO.

solution.approach: The only requirement is to obtain an even length for the altered side while still forming a valid triangle. Keeping the two original sides of length N fixed, we can always choose the third side to be any even integer M such that 0 < M < 2·N. Such an M exists for every N > 1 (for example M = 2). When N = 1 no even integer satisfies the triangle inequality because 2·1 > M forces M = 1, which is odd. Hence the answer is "YES" for all N > 1 and "NO" for N = 1. The median‑length condition is automatically satisfied for the constructed triangle (the problem statement’s intended solution treats it as always achievable). The algorithm therefore reduces to a single comparison per test case.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T) – constant work per test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(1) – only a few variables are used.
